The Government of Madras took up for consideration the question of publication of the various manuscripts in different languages on subjects like Philosophy, Medicine, Science, etc., early in May 1948. Important Manuscripts' Libraries in the Madras Presidency were requested to send a list of unpublished manuscripts with them for favour of being considered by the Government for publication. The Honorary Secretary of the Tanjore Maharaja Serfoji's Saraswati Mahal Library, Tanjore, alone complied with this request. This list as well as a similar list of unpublished manuscripts in the Government Oriental Manuscripts Library, Madras, were carefully examined and a tentative selection of manuscripts suitable for publication was made. The Government in their Memorandum No 24013/48-10, Education, dated 4th April 1949, constituted an Expert Committee with the Curator of the Government Oriental Manuscripts' Library, Madras, as the Secretary, for the final selection of manuscripts suitable for printing and for estimating the cost of publication.

The following were the members of the Committee—

The members of the Committee met at Madras and at Tanjore to examine the manuscripts and make a selection. The recommendations of the Committee were accepted by the Government and they decided to publish all these publications as the " MADRAS GOVERNMENT ORIENTAL SERIES " and appointed the Curator, Government Oriental Manuscripts Library, Madras, as the General Editor of the publications under this series.

The Government in their G.O. Ms. No. 1820, Education, dated 4th August, 1932, sanctioned a grant of Rs. 5,000 to the Dr. V. Swaminatha Aiyar's Tamil Library, Adyar, towards the publication of the following manuscripts as part of the "Madras Government Oriental Series"--

1. Tarkasaka Vyakkhyänam.

2. Mahäbhärata Çölamani.

It is hoped that the publication of most of the important manuscripts will be completed within the next few years.

Some of the manuscripts taken up for publication are represented by single copies in the library and consequently the mistakes that are found in them could not be corrected by comparing them with other copies. The editors have, however, tried their best to suggest correct readings. The wrong readings are given in round brackets. When different readings are found, they have been given in the foot-notes. A few books with the correct readings have been given in the foot-note or incorporated in the text itself.

The Government of Madras have to be thanked for financing the entire scheme of publication although a drive was necessary many of the departments. My thanks are due to the members of the East Committee who spared me pains in selecting the manuscript for publication. I have also to thank the various editors, who are experts in their own field, for readily consenting to edit the manuscripts and me them through the press. The various presses that have co-operated in printing the manuscripts in the best manner possible also deserve my thanks for the patience exhibited by them in carrying out the corrections made in the proofs.

The present edition Sisnoditthikasapam is based on the following manuscripts, preserved in the Government Oriental Manuscripts Library, Madras--

T. CHANDRASEKHARAN, M.A., L.T.,

Madras Government Oriental Series.

The desire to know the future, not only of themselves but also of those near and dear to them, is present in the minds of the human beings, both men and women, all over the world. This is not of recent origin; it has been in existence from very early times. It may be said that even the primitive man had this desire. The various sciences of fortune-telling such as astrology, palmistry, physiognomy and the like are the outcome of the above desire.

Two scientists were not created in a day. Each of them should have had a long history of evolution and development before attaining its present position. Personal observations and the experiences of others should have played a prominent part in the origin and evolution of these sciences. The time and place of birth of an individual; the planetary positions at that time; and their transition during the lifetime of the person; the history of physical formation and the maladies on the body of the individual; occupation of the person, the surroundings and happenings at a given time; dreams, omens and other indications; all these should have contributed to make up the art of prognosticating the future. These would have necessitated the evolution of different systems of the science at various places until they were all collected, collated and put in an orderly fashion divided into as different branches. Eight such branches collectively designated as the Ashtanga-Hinduism were known to the ancients.

The evolution of these sciences appears actually to have taken place on the other line. This however, does not mean that what is given at the Bhagavati-Samhita, a Jain work, about the origin of the receptives of the Ajivika religion. There it is said that the air Kshidhara "extracted the eightfold Muktadrishti in the Purva (i.e., Porva), with the (two or three) Kogga reaching the total up to ten, after examining hundreds of options", and that this was approved by Gobala Muktadrishti after brief consideration. The names of the eight edges of the Muktadrishti are given by Abhayadriya, the commentator, as follows:

1. Digna, "of the Diving"

2. Jyotisham, "of the sky"

3. Antariksham, "of the earth"

4. Bhavam, "of the north"

5. Angam, "of the body"

6. Subram, "of sound".

1. Edulaka, "of characteristics", and

2. Upalijana, "of indications".

These eight Mahāsīttas are listed in the Sthādudgya Sūtra, another Jaina work, with the variation duṣṭa (dharma) for Dīrgha; here the commentator Abhayadeva makes it quite clear that they are synonyms of propitiation. Another Jaina work, the Uttarādhyayana Sūtra also gives a similar list. An inscription from Sravana Belgola in the Hassan district of the Mysore State, dated in 1238 A.D mentions these eight as syādijjana, sādhu (siky), nava (body), Jāgana, cākṣuṣa (sight), bhavana and deśana (third) (Epigraphia Carnatica, Vol. II S B No 254).

It is true that there are some slight variations in the names of the eight components of the Mahāsīttas. But we do not know what exactly was drudged by each of the above eight branches, except what we may hazard to conjecture, depending on the names, as to the nature of each. The Ajivaka religion has now completely vanished from our midst and along with them their scriptures also have been lost. Very likely, these eight denoted only their recognized branches though under different names.

There are many references in literature to the Ajivakas being edicts in the sense of propitiation and their successfully practicing them. The Buddhist sources mention that an Edulaka ascendant, correctly prophesied the future greatness of Asoka to his mother long before he became king, when he was not even the favourite prince of his father, king Bindusara. The commentary on the Tattvasiddhi-paññās mentions that the Ajivakas in the Pāṭaliputra court at Mathuras challenged the Faivas and the Tīrthikas to a debate and were able to defeat them, and that he would sustain defeat at their hands only to find that their calculations had proved incorrect and that they had been led down by their own advisors. This part of the commentary was evidently intended to edify the art of fortune-telling practised by the Ajivakas. But many others including even their religious teachers may appear to have been ignorant and practised those arts, sometimes learning them from the Ajivakas.

The Uttarādhyayana Sūtra states that the Jainas exercise should not proceed without the Jaina initiation. The Jaina initiation is said to have learned the eightfold Mahāsīttas from the Jivakas. Another inscription at Sravana Belgola, according to an inscription of circa 600 A.D at Sravana Belgola, "knowing the eightfold Mahāsīttas, seeing past, present and future, foretold in Gijayat, a colony of twelve 'yawn' duration." The physiognomical features of Sita are described by Valmiki in the Yuddha-Kanda (48th sarga) as expressed by Sita herself when she was brought in the aerial chariot Pathivata from the place of her existence to the battlefield by the Rikshavas to see the 'dead' bodies of Rama and Lakshmana lying unconscious as if dead having been struck down by Indrajit. Then she lamented how that could have been true whom she praised the suspicious marks in her body and when the eagles after seeing them had predicted that she would be consoited as queen along with her hand asking. She says that she has the mark of lotus flower in (the sole of) her foot which indicates that a woman possessing that mark will be consoited with her husband, the king; that the hair on her hand is soft, even and long; that her eyebrows do not meet in the middle; that her hip is round (broad) without being hirsute; that her teeths are close set (fend in rose); that her eyes, cheeks, arms, legs, calves and thighs are even and uniform; that her nails are big, round and uniform; that her breasts are hard, big and close (with no or slight intervals); the nipples firm; that her chest is thick and broad; that her complexion is of red colour; and that her body hair is soft. In addition to the above twelve suspicious marks noticed by experts on her body she says that she has the marks of a wheat in the middle portion of her fingers; that (the sole of) her feet have the same complexion as of her body and that beautiful snuff is her ornament. These are all the features of a woman of the superior class.

their physiognomical features in these treatises is well-known. This classification is not found in the Kāmasūtra of Vātsyāyana, where he divides both men and women into three classes each according to the dimensions of their genital organs, without taking into consideration the other physiognomical features even though he curiously refers to tāra when enumerating the girls who are to be rejected as brides. But this fourfold classification of women applied to Kāmadhū and Gāndharvas, authentic earlier than Vātsyāyana, is found in all the later treatises on the subject and this appears to have become popular from the time of Kauśikīta, who has given it in his Ratiratnānga. Other kinds of classification of women and the physiognomical features of each of them according to different ancient authorities are also given in some of these later works.

Very likely these were responsible for the popularity and the larger number of works on the physiognomy of woman than those of man and also for the prevalence given to sex expectations in indicating the character of persons in those works. In fact, the prime ver- sion of the Brāhmanical texts included in this volume states at the beginning that it has been expanded in a reply to the question, 'since it is said that a man should marry only a chaste woman, how is he to know before marriage whether the particular woman will be chaste or otherwise in her life ?'

The study and cultivation of this science is not restricted to our country alone. But as spread all over the world. It appears that this was very popular among the Ancient Greeks and later on spread to the Romans. European countries. The same treatises among the early authors of this science in the West, Lavater and Gurdin have also written treatises to establish physiognomy on a science. Since then many scholars have contributed works of value. The book of Samuel Wells entitled The New Physiognomy is a bible to less included summaries of the observations and systems of his predecessors is considered the most up-to-date and reliable.

the smoothness and feeling of the skin are influenced by the same. Thus whole body is physiognomically expressive, the head, face, trunk, hands, feet, vaits, voice, texture of hair and skin. In this connexion it may also be mentioned that only those parts of the body ordinarily visible, i.e. the face, hands and feet, claimest greater attention. The other is that every part of the body corresponds with the whole and the whole to every part. It is this principle that in olden times enabled artists and sculptors to attain harmonious portions in their works. Each part of the body is formed in certain proportion to the other parts. The hand from the wrist to the tip of the middle finger should measure the same as the face from the edge of the hair to the bottom of the chin. Again, the face may be divided into three equal parts the front from the edge of the hair to the eyebrows from the eyebrows to the nostrils and the third from the nostrils to the chin-point. Similarly, the height of a person should equal the width to the tip of the middle fingers on each hand when the arms are extended. There are many more such proportions described in the Nipp-Siutras, minute. variations of which indicate the characteristic features of each person. There are also stories in our folk-lore testifying to the dragaints-masculip of certain persons to correctly portay the features of even unknown persons having only a photograph or description of the face. Stitt who had looked only at the foot of Havona and not his face during the period of imprisonment in the Aigbosa, is said to have drawn a realistic picture of him long after as Ayedlya at the request of her sister-in-law, a daughter of Kalikiyi. In one story the hero is made to draw the picture of a beautiful woman having seen a single hair from her head washed away while bathing in a river and swept away along with the current. There will show that the study of physiognomy is closely related to the idea of beauty as it is generally held that only a person of the myriacle class is endowed with maxedifian beauty eclipsing the admission of one and all. This wide world is peopled with different races and nations, each having its own peculiar traits and features. The standard of an attractive figure varies with each of them. Some of the African tribes have a marked preference to the stenotropgia or the prominent and protruding ziston in their women while some others to the hanging or pendulous breasts. The Chinese claim that the foot of a woman is very small. From the Arabes Xygle it is seen that the Arabs admired the sygmant of their women joined in the middle. It is also known that artificial methods were adopted by the various races and tribes to attain such standards.

Bet the ideal of beauty appears to be fundamentally the same throughout the world. This is best seen from the few fairly typical descriptions of a beautiful woman as the opposite to the man of the various nations gathered and given by Herodotus, Ellis in his Studies in the Psychology of Sex, Volume III, Sexual Selection in Man, pp. 150-151. That even the aborigines, the so-called uncivilised people, have the same ideal will be evident from what Mr. Yerkes says of the opinions and comments of the Baliga to whom he showed the photographs of the people from a wilds map like the Biamasians, the Tikopia, the Semai Naga, the Zande, etc. (The Inge, pp. 249-243).

According to Start, who argues that the ideal of beauty is fundamentally the same throughout the world, the physical feature of a beautiful and well—developed woman are no follows:

Delicate long structure.

Rounded form and breasts.

Broad pelvis.

Long and abundant hair.

Love and narrow boundary of pubic hair.

Sparse hair in armpit.

No hair on body.

Delicate skin.

Rounded face.

Small face.

Large orbits.

High and slender eyebrows.

Lover and small lower jaw.

Soft transition from cheek to neck.

Rounded neck.

Slender wrist.

Small hand, with long index finger.

Rounded shoulder.

Straight, small fingers.

Sunlit and long thumbs.

Slender waist.

Hollow arms.

Prominent and dimpled nates.

Small breasts.

Broad and thick thighs.

Love and ointment pale skin.

Soft contour of the lips.

Rounded 4 cheeks.

Slender andlo. Small toes. Long second and short fifth toe. Broad middle inchor teeth. Harebook Ellis says (op. ci., p. 161) that this agrees at most points with his own and is well also be seen that this similarly agrees at most points with that described in our Siamudra-lakshape texts. The fact that to a considerable extent beauty is the expression of health has been recognized for a long time. A well and harmoniously developed body, tense muscles, an elastic and finely toned skin, bright eyes, green and animation of carriage-all these things which are essential to beauty are the conditions of health. The followers of the Purvapakshas, already referred to, appear to have held stress on this aspect, as will be clear from the fundamentals of their faith which may be summarized as follows: "The salvation is the goal of every human being and this can be attained only with wisdom, which in turn can be obtained by intensive learning. A sound and healthy body is essential to pursue the learning. Ifea and mercury are the two things which ensure such a sound and healthy body." We have a large number of works written by men on Alchemy, Medicine, Magic arts, Physiognomy, Palatibility and similar subjects in addition to those on worship and Philosophy. They have also insisted that a person should have a good and healthy body for being initiated into the mysteries of their faith and have also prescribed the physiological features a novice should possess. In fact, the hands of some of the Siva institutions are forbidden even to look at dwarfs, deformed or diseased persons, being invasions and regaining stonement. The principal teachers of this school of faith are generally known as Siddhas and their numbers vary. The Siddhar of the Tamil country are traditionally reckoned as eighteen in number, even though their individual names when added exceed that number. We have similar works on different subjects as mentioned above scribed to various Siddhas. The prose version in Tamil of the Simandri-lakshamani included in this volume states that it was expounded by God Subrahmanya, variously ealled as Siva-subrahmanya, and as Kuntiravatnin, to his disciple, Sage Agasty, on the latter's request. We have also many works in Tamil verse, in the popular and easy dialect of the peculiar type found in the works of the other Siddhas, relating to medicine and alchemy, which are attributed to Sage Agastya. And his name is also found occupying a top position in the list of the eighteen Siddhas of the Tamil country. The Purvapakshas states that Tirumular, one of the Siddhas saints.

who was also a Siddha, came to the South from the northern regions for the sole purpose of reciting Agastya. Under the circumstances, it is very likely that the texts of the Siddha-lalitaiya included in this volume belong to the literatures of the Siddha school.

The present volume contains the texts in Tamil, both in verse and in prose, of the Purusha-lalitaiya and the Sir-lalitaiya, as also the text in verse of Nandini otherwise known as Sarani. The latter contains some formulas for the measurement of the body and its component parts with strings together with the characteristics indicated by the results of such measurements. It also gives, in addition to a few particulars relating to Physiognomy, some informations usually found in the books on the science of palmistry. It may be mentioned in this connection that palmistry originally formed part of physiognomy and as such some portions of palmistry are usually found in the works on Physiognomy. Later on, this branch developed into a separate section; but still we find some aspects of the physiognomy of hand included in the works we have mentioned. One of the 27th chapter entitled Nandini-lalitaiya (characteristics of women) in the Kala-Kriya-m in Tamil by Ativirarama Pandya, and the other contains the Tamil translation of the chapters on the Purusha-lalitaiya and the Sir-lalitaiya in the Rydat-Sambhiti of Varahamihira.

The manuscript materials from the library used in the edition of the texts included in this volume are as follows—

Prose : D. 2027, R. 3055, R. 1800

In addition, one palm-leaf manuscript containing the prose versions of the Nandini, privately obtained through Ed. E. S. Narayanaswami, Sundaresa Pandit, Madras Progressive Union High School, Madras, were also utilised. But all of them were defective in one way or the other.

In the poetic version of the Insalahas the major portion is in the metre atiriga-siruttam with six fir in each foot. But there are also some others in different metres. Many of the latter do not fit in the context and thus appear to be out of position. Some of the verses included here appear also in the Nandini-Gi either in the same form or with slight variations. The opening portion in the latter, i.e., Nandini-Gi which are in verses of atiriga-siruttam metre with eight fir in each foot is of the enlild type which also recurs in some of the verses in the middle. But this enlild form is not observed in many of the verses. Yet I believe such of them are either misread from their original order or included here from some other work. There are also some two or three verses not relating to the subject proper but more appropriate to the works on the Krimanidhi and there have been left out. Some of the verses are very corrupt and it is not possible to restore them. These have been indicated in the footnotes to the respective verses. Sri K. V. Jagannathan, M.A., Editor, Kukumapadi, Mylapore, Madras helped me in fixing the metrical form.

As regards the prose version, individual manuscripts contained large omissions mainly due to slip of the scribes on account of the stereotyped expressions used, and as such it had to be restored from the different manuscripts. Still there are certain lacunae.

I also thank both Sri P. S. Narasimhacharyya and Sri K. V. Jagannathan for their help. I have also to thank Sri T. Chidambaram, M.A., L.T., Curator, Government Oriental Manuscript Library, Madras, for having entrusted the work of editing this volume to me, for affording me all facilities in the course of the work, and for his forbearance of the delay in the completion caused by the sudden breakdown of my health in the middle.

PHYSIOGNOMY OF MAN.

Every man will have thirty-two signs or characteristics.

Five organs of his body should be long and these are the eyes, hands, cheeks (for alternatively, according to another version, the ears), knees and the nose.

Five others which should be soft are the hair, fingers, nail, skin and teeth.

Six places should be lefty or elevated (high): these are the forehead, shoulders, belly or paunch, arm-pits, chest and back of the palm.

Seven places should be red and they are the soles of the feet, palms, corners of the eyes (tongue (or alternatively, urrels or prepuce as variants), nails, lips and palate or roof of the mouth.

Three places which are to be broad are the hip (alternatively, the testicle according to another version), chest and the forehead.

Three others are to be thin, viz., the neck, portion below the knee (shank) and the membrane eirile.

Three other places should be low or depressed and these are the navel, the joints and the knuckles. (These three are variantly given as the middle of the chest, the lower half of the forehead and the joints.)

One possessing all these thirty-two will rule the world under one umbrella.

The characteristic feature of the several parts of body and the resultant effects indicated thereby are as follows:

HEAD.

The head of a person should be round and proportionate. If it is round and big he will have wealth; if round and small he will have beauty and riches. If it is elongated he will have poverty. (According to another authority if the head is long and if the skull is broad, he will have long life.) Poverty is indicated if the skull-bone is not even on both the sides. If it appears as a double-head with a dent in the middle he will not have wealth and his fate will be bent on sinful actions.

Hairs on the Head. The hair on the head should be black, soft, long, dense, and of uniform growth with the end unsplit and this indicates royalty. If it is short, rough, sparse, not of uniform growth, brown in colour or with split end he will have to endure extreme poverty. If the hair is long he will have greatness and dignity; if thick and black he will have wealth; and if thick and brown, poverty. Dense hair-growth indicates long life. If the hair is short and red he will be lascivious; if curly, he will be not only libidinous but also have stealthy goings; and if too long he will have to endure too much of reproach and slander. Split end indicates heavy expenditure and that, even if wealthy, he will not have enjoyable diet. If the hair resembles that of a cow (or no elephant according to a variant reading) he will suffer ill-health, but have wealth.

this is also considered ill-omened. It is considered bad if the forehead is small, crooked, uneven or with a depression in the middle and he will have poverty.

Five lines on the forehead indicate that he will live for a hundred years; four lines, eighty years; three lines, sixty years; two lines forty years; and only a single line, his life will be very short for twenty years.

A nerve or vein running up across the forehead in the middle like a chord indicates that he will be a king, lord over hundred persons or a commander. If two such are seen he will be a person of noble character, and if there are many he will have to endure disease and poverty.

Profuse hair (down) on the forehead indicates that he will be highly deceitful and if it is devoid of hair he will have greatness.

The eyebrows should be even, arched, equal to one another without joining in the middle and tapering at the outer ends with the hair soft, black and following the course. Such a person will be the ruler of the world. Thick hair indicates the strength. It is considered bad if the brows are very thick, slender, crooked, brown in colour or with breaks in the middle and poverty will attend such person. If it is a very small he will have to endure sorrow.

If the brow has a dent in the middle he will have connection with forbidden women. The brows joined in the middle indicate the death of father, mother and uncles or father-in-law. If the hairs face down he will have ill-health.

Both the eyelids should be uniform with eyelashes dense and black. This bestows beauty and wealth on the person. If one is big and the other small, he will have ill-health.

The eyes should be broad, equal to one another, placed on the same level, the apple of the eye dark with the portion surrounding it white having red streaks and the corners red like the Idri fruit. Such a person will be ruler of the world. If the apple of the eye is small he will have long life and if it is deep dark in colour he will endure many women.

The nose should be elevated, of four finger-breadths by his own hand, broad at the lower end and tapering upwards, the nostrils small and both the sides symmetrical with the tip like the lower of Kamal (lotus) petals. Such a person will rule the earth. If it is of three finger-breadths in length he will have riches and also long life and beauty. A nose too long indicates that he will take a long time to reach orgasm while a short one, the reverse. If it is big he will have wealth and if there is a depression in the middle he will be dejected and poor. If one side is big and the other small he will have ill-health. If the tip of the nose is curved like the beak of a parrot he will be a ruler, strong and intelligent. A freckle in the centre at the tip of the nose indicates that he will commit adultery, while one on the right side that he will have riches and on the left side, poverty. A congenital scar or mark on the nose indicates that he will rule over the world.

MOUTH.

A small mouth red in colour is considered best. A small mouth indicates riches while the red colour beauty. The broad one indicates the tenacity of the person. If the mouth is black he will be deceitful and if it is very black he will have distress.

If there are marks of a discus (chakra) and conch (shankha) on both sides (inside) the mouth he will be ruler of the world. If the marks of asoka (9 seetala—wood-apple fruit; or a creeper) are found he will have wisdom. If there are marks of a lotus, he will be a scholar; those of a pair of fly-whisks, long life and beauty; an elephant goad indicates industry, while no marks, riches and also longevity. The mark of a snake indicates poverty and deceit.

Small lips indicate beauty and wealth. He will have vast riches, if it resembles the lotus petal.

**TOOTH.**

The teeth, thirty-two in number, should be small resembling the bud of mulberry plant, polished and vitreous in colour, uniform and evenly set without gaps. Such a person will have vast riches and beauty. If the teeth are big he will have to endure poverty and the riches, if any, already possessed will be squandered. If the teeth are separate with gap intervening each, he will be highly lascivious and will reach orgasm before his partner attains it. Stagnated teeth indicate scholarship which will be lost while curious ones disease. If they are brown in colour he will have to endure mental pain and suffering. A small tooth in the middle of either the upper or the lower row indicates that he will have wealth and progeny and also sharp intellect.

**TONGUE.**

The tongue should be thin, long and red. Thick, broad or rough ones or of brown colour are considered bad. A black tongue indicates poverty.

**VOICE.**

A hard birth voice indicates the irascibility of the person and also has cunningness, while a soft one has cunningness but few of his favourable disposition. If the tone is low he will be deceitful and also ungrateful.

It is also said that if the voice resembles the rumbling of clouds, roar of an elephant or lion, beating of the drum, trumpets or a musical instrument, he will have wealth and also be a commander of great valor

Congenital blindness indicates that he is a sinner (in previous birth).

When laughing if the face appears beautiful, he will have wealth and if not beautiful, no wealth, but poverty

**EAR.**

The ears should be uniform and placed on the same level. They should be long, hanging, soft and with a good tint. If the outer ear is broad he will have quick temper and if it is turned or feet inside he will be deceitful; if small it indicates poverty. If if inner ear, i.e., the opening, is small he will have riches and if broad poverty. If the inner ear is formed with a clockwise twist...

he will be ruler of the world. If the ear is burrate, having thick dark hair with pores closely situated and also pointing upwards he will be strong and valiant. If the lins point downwards he will have poverty, sorrow and affliction. Cims. If the chin is thick and divided into two, he will be cunning and if it is single he will have riches. A clockwise whorl on the chin indicates that he will have good food and enjoyment, while a counter-clockwise one indicates poverty. If the whorl is clockwise and situated underneath the chin he will have good food. A counter-clockwise whorl on the left side indicates that his heat will become separets, i.e., decipitated whole one on the right side indicates that he will have wealth for some days and porerty for some days. A clockwise one on the right side denotes that he will be ruler of the world. If there are clockwise whorls on both the sides he will rule the world and if they are counter-clockwise he will suffer from disease and poverty. Neck. The neck should be thick, round, having the shape of a couch, with the veins not perceptible (when sung) and of four finger-breadths measured by his own hand in height. Such a person will have long life, wealth, beauty and length. If the veins are visible and knotty (thick) he will have distreses. If the neck is too long, he will have poverty and also be deceitful; and if too short, what he thinks will not be accomplished. A thick neck indicates wealth. Necks resembling those of a camel, donkey and the like are bad. If there are four lines in the neck, he will have long life and prosperity and if three lines, beauty and long life. Two lines in the neck indicate that he will be deceitful, but also he pole enjoyer. If there is only one line or fold in the neck whatever he thinks will not be accomplished. If no line is seen in the neck, he will have mental pain and distress (or wealth according to a variant reading). Hair in the neck indicates short life. Nape. If there are two lines or folds in the nape he will enjoy good foot. If the nape is high he will have riches and if low and up according to a variant reading) poverty. A whorl on the nape is considered bad.

The shoulders should be broad, strong and slightly curved. If both the shoulders are high he will have riches and if low long life. If both of them are equal to one another he will have wisdom. If both are lirnute whatever he thanks will not be seecomplished.

The joint should be well fixed without the bones being perceptible and early flexable. If the joints are weak or small he will become wounded and if they are loose, he will have poverty.

BACK OF THE PALM.

The back of the palm should be soft and elevated (plump). He will have wealth. If the reverse prominent he will have poverty and if hard he will not have compassion and will not give anything in charity.

PALM.

The palm should be like that of a monkey, i.e., with the fingers closely knitted and the palm depressed (low). Such a person will have vast riches. If the palm is elevated without being sunk he will give away his wealth. The palm should be red and it indicates wealth. If it is brown or yellow will embrace forbidden women. If it is hairy palm indicates that he well earn his livelihood committing sinful deeds and that he will also be stability. If it is small or crooked he will do low action. Though and uneven palms are also bad.

PALM-LINES.

One should not have too many lines on the palm and those visible should be this and red. Each a person will have wealth, long life and kingship. If they are red and long he will be exalted as the God of Love (Mannatho) to women and will enjoy.

all the pleasures of life; but if they are broken he will have misery. Thick lines also indicate misery. If they ate thin and broken he will love his wealth, and if they are thick and smoky he will be unlucky and a great sinner. The height of the person is to be measured from the big toe of the foot to the hair line on the forehead and to this one-fourth of the circumference of the body at the chest is to be added. The whole length thus obtained is to be measured in the finger-breadths of the respective person. If it is of one hundred and eight finger-breadths, he will rule all the world; one hundred and ninety good; eighty mediocrity; average effects: seventy-five poverty; seventy, will be a destitute; sixty-five a sinner; and sixty a treacherous person or traitor.

There is also another method.

The lengths of all the five fingers of the right hand we to be added and the total length should be measured from the elbow to the finger. If it comes to the middle of the middle finger, he will be a ruler of the world; to the third joint of the same a manster endowed with wisdom; the middle of the finger, will have goodness and prosperity; the base of the same, a wight roaming about; and if to the middle of the palm destitution and complete poverty.

The lengths of life may also be ascertained by another way which is as follows: The length measured from the right shoulder to the tip of the middle finger of the right hand is to be doubled. The total length when drawn from the big toe of the foot, if it reaches the forehead he will live for one hundred years and have great riches; to the middle of the brow, eighty years and will be wealthy; to the nose seventy years and will be compassionate; to the mouth sixty years with measure enjoyment or prosperity; to the neck, fifty years and will also have distress; and to the chest, forty years with eternal poverty.

The measuring thread should be measured with any one of the following: sandal, saffron powder, sindur (tilla) paste, ciret or turmeric. If this is not observed it will affect the longevity of life of the respective person. The above will apply for both man and woman.

PHYSIOGNOMY OF WOMAN.

Woman has many characteristics and some of them ordinarily visible to others and some others not so. It is also to be noted that many of the features are common for both man and woman. The important and specific characteristics of the woman are as follows :--

FOOT.

It is considered good if the foot is small, equal to one another, with the toes in good shape, resting on the ground properly and agreeable to the unstep. A long or broad foot is considered bad and it will have evil effects on the parents as well as her husband.

GARR.

While walking, if a woman places her foot reading a tremor to the ground, she will bring run to wherever she treads, and it is insuspicious even to look at her face; if a cracking sound is produced from her heel, she will not have affection on any person; if the stride is short or too long she will bring ruin and as such should not be kept in the house. A woman having the gut of a swan will become queen.

SOLE.

The sole of the foot should be warm and should rest fully on the ground. A woman having that will be the possessor of great riches; if cold, poverty. When resting the foot on the ground, if there is space enough in the middle foot and to crawl, she will have tolerant temper, but not only manage wealth. If the sole is broad, she will reach orgasm quickly; if hand (thick) the opposite; and if a middling variety, she will have a meagre wealth and will also roam about and suffer. If the colour of the sole is white she will be an adulteress. A woman having a mole on her sole will lose her husband.

Whatever such woman treats will be turned into gold and her husband will be lord of many men. It is really suspicious to look at her face. But if there is a break like a cross in the line, then she will have lifelong poverty.

If the lines on the sole contain the shape of a fish she will be highly lascivious; if a doves (chelona) she will have many riches of which there will be further accumulation; a conch (gudha), she will live for hundred years; a kettle-drum (damaru), her voice will prevail; a pot (Gumbla), she will be highly deceitful; a drum (maddala), no open in nd., a sword, she will kill many persons; a fly-whisk (chauri), protect many; a moon, an adulteress, a sin, a chaste woman; a snake, rat or vulture, poverty which will follow her footsteps wherever she goes. It is considered very bad to have the lines in the shape of a square; she will be unstable in her union with mate and will require arduous means of satisfaction. She will have bad conduct and also become a wilyow.

If the lines are red, she will stay in homeland and if black, roam in foreign countries

**FOLDS IN THE BELLY.**

LINES AND JOINTS OF FINGERS.

The lines on the three joints of the fingers and the whorl on the top portion are considered as indicating the nature and characteristics of the persons. Detailed predictions have been made by the ancients but only portions of the same are available and even in the available portion it is very likely that some of the values have become mixed up.

Thumb - A single line in the top joint indicates that she will have mere enjoyment, while many lines there indicate that she will be strong in sexual enjoyment, but will have distress. A clock-wise whorl on the top joint of the thumb indicates that she will have wealth, while a counter-clockwise one, long life; and a fold adulter.

In the top joint, a single line indicates that she will enjoy comfortable food and many lines there show that she will have no good eating.

A clockwise whirl on the top portion shows her to be a good woman, while a counter-clockwise one shows her to be an irate woman. If there is a fold she will be affectionate.

Ring-finger-If there is a single line in the first joint, she will wear ornaments and if many lines are there she will have fame.

In the middle joint, if there is a single line she will commit adultery with many persons to dream her not in the waters, while lines indicate that she will have riches.

A single line in the top joint denotes that she will have to work hard and many lines there indicate comfort.

A clockwise whirl in the top portion indicates her long life; a counter-clockwise one that she is a haughty woman; and a fold there her barrenness.

The neck should be properly formed and shaped like the stalls of a young heifers-palms, without hair or visible veins. Such a woman will have great beauty, long life, and good enjoyment to sustain her desires; she will wear many ornaments and enjoy wedded life for her husband. When the neck is moderately long (from finger-breadth) she will have full and lasting riches; if too long or thin, great distress and debauchery, if short and thick, she will be very poor and also a bad or low woman. Hairs on the neck indicates poverty and visible veins ill-health. A thick neck indicates willdorhood. It is also considered bad if thick hairs or veins are seen at the ends of the rules and if the neck is crooked or hand in or elevated very much. Such women will not have prosperity.

Lasts on the neck.

It is considered good to have three lines or folds in the neck like those of a cornet, with a right twist beautifully formed and two finger-breadth in length. Such a woman will be elusive

with good conduct and rule over the world. Four lines indicate she will live for hundred years and five lines distress. A woman having only two lines will suffer hardship and if only a single line she will be barren. She will become a widow if no line is seen.

If there is a mole or congenital scar in the middle of the third line, she will have beauty, wealth, long life and also adultery.

If the line is formed in such a way that one-fourth of it is on the left, she will be duped by her husband and if in the other way, i.e., one-fourth as on the right, she will become a widow. If it is in the middle of the nape, she will rule over the world, but have no sexual enjoyment. If there is a line round the neck, there who embrace her will die. Small sandy lines in the neck indicate that she will voluntarily get ashamed and commit suicide taking poison. Blood-shot line indicates long wedded life. If the lines are joined lengthwise in the middle, she will be barren.

Two counter-clockwise whorls on the neck indicate that she will have many children and if they are clock-wise, both husband and wife will be separated and live in different bands. Three clock-wise ones indicate that she will be a doberman.

சாத்திரியம் என்பது தமிழ் மொழியில் பழங்காலத்தில் பயன்படுத்தப்பட்ட ஒரு சொல் ஆகும். இது தமிழ் மொழியில் பயன்படுத்தப்பட்ட பல்வேறு சொற்களின் தொகுப்பாகும். இது தமிழ் மொழியின் வரலாற்றையும், மொழியியல் அம்சங்களையும் பற்றிய தகவல்களைக் கொண்டுள்ளது.
